card.intuition: Test-Driven Development (TDD) is a process where you write a failing test first, then write the minimum code to pass it, and finally clean up the code.
card.analogy: Building a Lego set with a missing piece detector: first, you look for a specific piece you don't have (Red: Failing Test). Then, you find that exact piece and put it in (Green: Pass). Finally, you straighten the blocks so they look nice (Refactor).
card.time_complexity: O(T) tests to run per cycle
when_to_use: Ensuring high test coverage from the start of a project
Designing clean APIs by thinking about how they are 'called' before how they are 'built'
Preventing regressions when adding new features to complex logic
gotchas: Don't skip the Refactor step! If you only do Red and Green, your codebase will eventually become a messy pile of hacks.
TDD is not about Testing; it is about DESIGN. It helps you write decoupled, modular code.

steps.annotation: Red: write a failing test first. It defines the desired behaviour before any implementation.
Green: write the minimum code to make the test pass. No extras — just enough.
Refactor: clean up duplication and improve design. Tests stay green throughout.
Repeat: write the next failing test. The cycle drives the entire implementation.

exercise.prompt: Identify the three steps of the TDD cycle in order.
exercise.starter_code: cycle = ["___ (Write failing test)", "_____ (Make it pass)", "________ (Clean up code)"]
hints: Stop light colors, then spruce up
exercise.test_code:
assert "red" in solution.lower()
assert "green" in solution.lower()
assert "refactor" in solution.lower()
print("PASS")
